rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute anxiety-driven somatic panic crisis with intractable vomiting and acute epigastric pain, carrying an organic differential (acute surgical abdomen / pancreatitis / gastroenteritis) that a telehealth clinician CANNOT exclude, plus a dehydration risk from persistent vomiting and an elevated self-harm risk (hopelessness / 'burden to family'). The source note itself required urgent out-of-hours face-to-face assessment to exclude a surgical abdomen and prevent dehydration. The telehealth-correct disposition is urgent in-person assessment today (T4 — ED / urgent care, self-transport feasible) to examine the abdomen, rehydrate and assess psychiatric safety, while validating the symptoms as real and coaching paced breathing. It is NOT T5/000 baseline because the patient reports no haematemesis, no collapse and no localising/tearing pain, and can self-transport — 000 is reserved for the escalation edges. It is NOT T2/T3-routine: dismissing this as 'just a panic attack' and deferring to a routine review is precisely the dismissive under-triage failure the case is built to detect. Under-triage is weighted 3× over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, or passing black tarry stools
escalation_edges.rationale: Blood in the vomit or melaena signals an upper-GI bleed (e.g. a Mallory-Weiss tear from forceful retching) — a time-critical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you bring up blood or dark grainy 'coffee-ground' material, or pass black tarry poo, call 000 straight away and tell them you're vomiting blood.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe, tearing or steadily worsening/localising abdominal pain, or a hard rigid tummy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 / go to ED now
escalation_edges.rationale: These change the picture from retching-soreness to a possible acute surgical abdomen (perforation, pancreatitis, obstruction) requiring emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the tummy pain suddenly becomes severe or tearing, spreads or gets worse, or your belly goes hard and rigid, call 000 or get to an emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down, or signs of dehydration (very dizzy, not passing urine, feeling faint)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / within the next few hours — be assessed in person
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent vomiting with no oral tolerance risks dehydration and electrolyte disturbance and needs in-person antiemetic and IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep even small sips of fluid down over the next few hours, or you feel very dizzy or aren't passing urine, get seen in person today for fluids.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting, collapse or loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope/collapse in the setting of vomiting and autonomic arousal is an emergency requiring ambulance assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel like you're going to pass out, or you do faint or collapse, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Active thoughts or a plan to end your life or harm yourself, or feeling unable to stay safe
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 or a crisis line and do not stay alone
escalation_edges.rationale: An active plan or intent, or an inability to stay safe in the acute identity-linked crisis, is a psychiatric emergency requiring immediate crisis response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start feeling like you might act on thoughts of harming yourself, or you can't keep yourself safe, call 000 or Lifeline 13 11 14 right now, and try to have someone with you — you don't have to get through this alone.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Sexual/gender-minority patient in acute distress, spending significant energy concealing their identity and fearful of being 'seen'
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Use consistently affirming, non-judgemental language; validate the symptoms and the identity as real; anticipate and address the fear of being seen when arranging in-person assessment; do not pathologise or moralise about the identity. Affirming communication materially increases the chance the patient accepts the needed escalation.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is avoiding a partner's calls; tends to isolate to avoid identity coming up
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Gently explore whether a trusted person (including the partner, at the patient's choice) can be with them or accompany them; reinforce not being left alone while self-harm risk is elevated and while actively vomiting.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: First, let's slow your breathing together — long, gentle breaths out — and the cramping in your hands will ease off by itself; it's frightening but not dangerous. What you're feeling is real: an intense stress reaction can genuinely make you vomit and cramp up. Because you've got stomach pain with a lot of vomiting, a clinician needs to feel your tummy in person today to be sure it isn't something in your abdomen — that can't be done over a screen. Please get to an emergency department or urgent care today; if you'd like, someone you trust can go with you. You are not overreacting, and you don't have to attend the event tonight. Sip small amounts of fluid in the meantime. If you vomit blood or dark grainy material, get sudden severe or tearing tummy pain, or feel faint — call 000. And if the feeling of being a burden or of not wanting to be here gets stronger, or you can't keep yourself safe, call 000 or Lifeline 13 11 14 straight away and don't be alone.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend an emergency department or urgent care in person today (a trusted person to accompany if possible); call 000 immediately for the emergency triggers above
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Emergency departments and 000 are available 24 hours; do not wait for clinic hours given the vomiting, the stomach pain and the level of distress.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the in-person assessment / discharge summary to the usual GP for review within 48 hours, to arrange affirmative psychological support and a mental health plan, review the acute crisis and coping strategies, and follow up any abdominal findings.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the intractable vomiting, epigastric pain and self-harm risk as 'just a panic attack / stress / minority stress' and advising home management or a routine review — premature psychosocial closure ('medical gaslighting').
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A missed surgical abdomen or pancreatitis deteriorates, the patient becomes dangerously dehydrated, or the elevated self-harm risk escalates to an attempt, because the presentation was invalidated and not assessed in person.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging urgent same-day in-person review but not ED-level assessment — acceptable only when the abdominal and self-harm screens are clearly reassuring and same-day access is assured; risks delayed exclusion of an organic abdomen or delayed rehydration if access slips.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 ambulance as the baseline when the patient reports no haematemesis, collapse or tearing pain and can self-transport — unnecessary ambulance use and added distress; mild, and far preferable to under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an acute surgical abdomen or pancreatitis is present cannot be resolved over telehealth, and the self-harm risk can only be partially gauged remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the uncertainty honestly, validate the symptoms as real, and resolve it by arranging urgent in-person assessment plus a direct self-harm screen — escalating to 000 for the emergency triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Resolving the uncertainty by assuming the benign panic/psychosocial explanation and managing at home, or by falsely reassuring the patient that their abdomen is fine.
